Title: Role of Anthocyanidin Reductase, Encoded by BANYULS in Plant Flavonoid Biosynthesis

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DFR-like NAD(P)H-dependent reductase of the flavonoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Condensed tannin branch downstream of anthocyanidin formation
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream, immediately downstream of ANS; branch point competing with anthocyanin formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms flavan-3-ol monomer units for condensed tannin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces anthocyanidins to their corresponding 2,3-cis-flavan-3-ols using NADPH or NADH
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-switch enzyme for directing flux from anthocyanins toward condensed tannins; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Arabidopsis Col-0 for AtBAN background; NA for M. truncatula
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Nicotiana tabacum; Arabidopsis thaliana; Escherichia coli
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Col-0 (Arabidopsis); NA for tobacco and E. coli
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Cauliflower mosaic virus 35S promoter (constitutive) for ectopic expression in tobacco and Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: MtBAN strongly expressed in young developing seeds, weakly in open flowers and flower buds, very weakly in leaves; AtBAN expressed in seed endothelium at onset of fertilization through preglobular embryo stage
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High ectopic transcript accumulation in leaves of transgenic tobacco and Arabidopsis under 35S promoter
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed coat/endothelium in Arabidopsis; petals in transgenic tobacco; leaves in transgenic Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Beneficial effects on animal and human health; antioxidant activity; contributes to flavor and astringency; protects ruminants against pasture bloat and improves nitrogen nutrition
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes condensed tannin accumulation and reduces anthocyanin pigmentation when ectopically expressed
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein enzyme assays in E. coli with anthocyanidin substrates; HPLC, HPLC-MS, GC-MS, NMR, and CD identification of products; constitutive overexpression in tobacco and Arabidopsis; DMACA staining; butanol-HCl CT quantification; mutant inference from ban and ans/tt18 phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ANS (functional pathway competition, not direct physical interaction demonstrated)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Ectopic expression causes loss of anthocyanin pigmentation in tobacco petals, indicating metabolic flux diversion from anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering BAN could enable condensed tannin production in forage legumes such as alfalfa for bloat safety, improved protein utilization, reduced greenhouse gas and urine-N losses, and modification of health properties, flavor, and astringency in crops
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Random sequencing of a Medicago truncatula seed cDNA library; no global omics accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Earlier hypothesis that BAN encoded leucoanthocyanidin reductase was disproven. Identical activities were observed for AtBAN and MtBAN. Minor trans products likely arose from epimerization artifacts. BAN exists as a single copy in the M. truncatula genome
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis BAN and Medicago truncatula BAN encode anthocyanidin reductase rather than the previously hypothesized leucoanthocyanidin reductase. 2. Recombinant BAN converts cyanidin, pelargonidin, and delphinidin into corresponding 2,3-cis-flavan-3-ols, including (-)-epicatechin. 3. Constitutive expression of BAN in tobacco petals and Arabidopsis leaves decreases anthocyanin accumulation and induces condensed tannin accumulation. 4. BAN functions immediately downstream of ANS and diverts flavonoid flux toward proanthocyanidin biosynthesis.
